text: The surgeon introduces a liver retractor to elevate the left lobe of the liver, followed by dissection of adhesions between the stomach and liver. The dissection continues between the previous Nissen fundoplication and the liver, using blunt and sharp techniques to separate the Nissen wrap from the esophagus, aided by a nasogastric tube for identification. Adhesions between the diaphragm and Nissen are dissected, creating a window for transecting the Nissen wrap with a green load stapler, avoiding previous clips to prevent misfire. The procedure progresses with dissection at the angle of His.

The surgeon dissects and removes adhesions from previous surgeries, focusing on the posterior aspect of the stomach. A gastric pouch is created on the lesser curvature using a blue load stapler, followed by further dissection of adhesions. The angle of His is defined from the posterior aspect of the gastric pouch, and the remaining gastric pouch is transected using a green load stapler due to the thickness of the tissue.

The surgeon places clips for additional security after creating the gastric pouch. The transverse colon and mesocolon are elevated to locate the ligament of Treitz, exposing the inferior mesenteric vein and pancreas. The small bowel is measured approximately forty centimeters distal from the ligament of Treitz and transected using a white load vascular stapler.

The surgeon places a clip on the biliopancreatic limb to confirm its identity, then runs it back to the ligament of Treitz. The alimentary limb is brought up to the gastric pouch, and the surgeon begins constructing a two-layer gastro-jejunal anastomosis using 2-0 Vicryl sutures, focusing on the posterior layer.

After completing the posterior layer of the gastro-jejunal anastomosis, the surgeon creates a gastrotomy in the gastric pouch and an enterotomy in the alimentary limb using a Harmonic Scalpel. The second layer of stitching is performed using Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon completes the second posterior layer of the gastro-jejunal anastomosis, taking separate stitches at the corners for secure bites. A 32 French tube is passed into the small bowel to calibrate the anastomosis, ensuring the posterior layer is not compromised. The third layer is constructed, followed by a fourth layer using 2-0 Vicryl stitches.

The surgeon tests the anastomosis by submerging it underwater and insufflating air to check for leaks. After confirming no leaks, the alimentary limb is measured to a length of one hundred centimeters to prepare for the jejunal jejunostomy. An anchor stitch is placed between the alimentary limb and the biliopancreatic limb, followed by creating an enterotomy in each limb. The anastomosis is performed using a sixty millimeter white load stapler.

The surgeon closes the infratomy with two micro-stitches and uses a silk suture to close the mesenteric defects, carefully avoiding mesenteric vessels. The mesenteric defect between the bowel loops and the mesentery is completely closed. A final endoscopy is performed, and the anastomosis is submerged underwater to check for leaks and ensure patency, concluding the procedure.
